episode_summary: The hosts chat about aviation waypoints and pilot/air-traffic quirks, a 17th‑century bone transplant using a dog’s bone and the resulting excommunication, a world record involving model trains pulling a full-size train, and the old tradition of “telling the bees” about family events. Along the way they explore related oddities from church law to model railway culture and ancient bee lore.

facts.fact: There is a global database of over 37,000 aviation waypoints, many of which have humorous or tribute names — including Looney Tunes references near Portsmouth, New Hampshire, NIMOY near Boston where Leonard Nimoy was born, and SNOOPY near Charles Schulz's hometown.
OK, my fact this week is that the first ever bone transplant used a dog's bones to repair a man's head. The patient was immediately excommunicated by the church for no longer being fully human.
My fact is, there is a world record for pulling a train with model trains.

My fact this week is that in the 19th century, if the owner of an estate died, it was traditional for the estate's beekeeper to inform all of the bees of the death. And then allow them to mourn by covering the hives in black veils.
